subject: [PATCH 0/5] allocate extent_buffer GFP_NOFAIL with unlocked retry

reviews.author: Johannes Thumshirn
reviews.summary: reviewer noted that alloc_extent_buffer() calls __alloc_extent_buffer() and init_extent_buffer(), which is inconsistent, and requested to either remove one of these calls or refactor the code
reviews.raw_body:
This looks wrong to me. Not the split out code, but how it is used in 
alloc_extent_buffer(). Either you keep calling __alloc_extent_buffer() 
in alloc_extent_buffer() OR don't call init_extent_buffer() in 
__alloc_extent_buffer(). I see this is an intermediate step (and I 
haven't looked at the rest of the series yet) but it looks wrong.

reviews.author: Boris Burkov (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged that the __alloc_extent_buffer function should not be called from alloc_extent_buffer, as it's meant to be used elsewhere; agreed to restructure in a future version (v2) and asked for feedback on deleting or open coding this function.
reviews.raw_body:
The idea is that you eventually (optionally) have the allocation happen
elsewhere, so alloc_extent_buffer should not call __alloc_extent_buffer
any more. That should be clearer in the second patch, I think.

I left __alloc_extent_buffer for btrfs_clone_extent_buffer() and
alloc_dummy_extent_buffer(). Would you prefer me to delete
__alloc_extent_buffer and open code it in those two callers?

Thanks,
Boris

2026-06-25.patch_summary: This patch series addresses contention and stalls in the Linux kernel caused by btrfs extent_buffer allocations being made while holding locks. The patches modify the allocation of extent_buffers to use GFP_NOWAIT, which allows the allocation to fail and retry outside of the critical section with GFP_NOFS | GFP_NOFAIL. This approach is analogous to how extent_buffers are read, where locks must be dropped before accessing the data. Although the series does not completely eliminate lock-holding allocations, it significantly reduces contention by allowing small cached slab allocations to occur outside of the critical path.

reviews.author: Johannes Thumshirn
reviews.summary: reviewer initially had concerns about the patch but after re-examining the complete series, they found it made sense and apologized for their previous noise
reviews.raw_body:
Yes looking at it again with the complete series applied it make more 
sense. Sorry for the noise.

2026-06-29.patch_summary: This patch series addresses contention and stalls in the Linux kernel caused by btrfs extent_buffer allocations being made while holding locks. The patches modify the allocation of extent_buffers to use GFP_NOWAIT, which allows the allocation to fail and retry outside of the critical section with GFP_NOFS | GFP_NOFAIL. This approach is analogous to how extent_buffers are read, where locks must be dropped before accessing the data. Although the series does not completely eliminate lock-holding allocations, it significantly reduces contention by allowing small cached slab allocations to occur outside of the critical path.
